passback_explanation: Shoes Though footbag can be played barefoot or with any kind of shoe, there are certain kinds that are most-suited to the sport. Among the many brands and models used over the years, the two that have most become part of the culture are the Adidas Rod Laver and Quantums. For a long time, from the mid-90s to mid-2010s, Lavers were the standard freestyle shoe, but the specific model that was popular is no longer made. Quantums were the first shoes designed specifically for footbag by the community itself which had a run in the 2010s and have recently been redesigned and released.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Tricks Footbag tricks are performed using a footbag and various parts of the body, most prominently the legs. To practice a trick without the bag, to get a feel for the motions, is to air shred it.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: As much as possible, the terms in this document are defined from simple to complex. Terms defined later in the document often build upon ones from earlier in the document. The first use of a term is bolded, while further uses of it are in normal text. Alphabetical index of all terms can be found at the end of the document.

passback_explanation: Alpine + [base trick] means to divide the trick between its uptime and downtime components (as long as it has both) and insert a duck or dive in between. Taking Ripwalk as the base trick (Stepping op Butterfly), Alpine Ripwalk is a Stepping Ducking Butterfly. Alpine can be applied to a nickname or technical name, so you could say Alpine Ripwalk or Alpine Stepping op Butterfly to mean the same thing.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: When analyzing the execution of a trick, the attack is how quickly the player begins the component of the next phase. Ex: When first learning Blur, players often need to attack the Mirage more quickly after the Stepping set.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Linking the same trick together two or more times can be described as back-to-back (b2b). Ex: PLO b2b = PLO>PLO.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Backside (BS) + [base trick] means to change the downtime component to symposium. Ex: BS Atomsmasher = Atomic Symp Mirage.
notes: matches UX1 token: Backside (body modifier; +1)

passback_explanation: Spins are most commonly performed as backspins where the body turns away from the bag and the back passes the bag first. An inspin turns towards the bag and the chest passes the bag first. Unless the prefix in- is used, a spin is presumed to be a backspin. In can be applied to Spinning, Gyro, or even full trick names to change the direction of the spin (like In-Vortex to mean In-gyro Drifter).
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A bailed trick is one that was attempted but modified on the fly to something easier to avoid dropping (ex: was going for a Spinning Whirl but bailed to Spinning Clipper because the set was too low). A footed trick is one that was almost hit, but the bag rolled off the stalling foot at the end. A dropped trick could also be over-dexed if the leg dexes too far which causes it to miss the bag entirely.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Circle and Battle are variants of a straight-ahead technical showdown. Circle mimics the style of a casual circle, while Battle is 1-on-1. Shred-Off is an online-only async variant of Battle.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: ADD Groupings Based on how many ADDs a trick has, it can be grouped and sorted into various tiers: 2-ADD and up tricks are referred to as Tiltless tricks. 3-ADD and up as Guiltless, 4 and up as Tripless, 5 and up as Fearless, 6 and up as Beastly, and 7 and up as Godly. For ADD tiers 2-5, the names can drop the “-less” suffix to describe the tier below them, for example: “Tilts” refers to 1-ADD tricks, “Guilts” 2-ADD, and so on. There are also special names for 3-trick Beastly and Godly combos: a 6>6>6 is called a “Beast”, and a 7>7>7 is called a “Jackpot”.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: There are alternate terms to refer to individual symposium dexes within a trick: alpha, beta, and gamma. For example: Beta Atomsmasher to mean Atomic Symp Mirage. (IMO) these terms are unnecessary and overly complicated; the above terms should make it obvious in the majority of cases which aspect of a trick is symposium.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Because toe and clipper are the most common contacts, there are two terms to describe a player’s preference for the sides of those. A left/right bigfoot player is one whose strong toe and clipper use the same foot. A left/right sideways player is one whose strong toe and clipper use the same side.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer
no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Within the guiltless tier are BOPs, which stand for the tricks “Butterfly”, “Osis”, and “Pdx Mirage”, which are the most basic guiltless tricks and the foundation for traditional guiltless play. Genuine Guiltless or just Genuine is a combo or string of Guiltless tricks that do not use BOPs, which is generally much more difficult.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Both sides or bs refers to doing a trick using both sides of the body. A combo/string/player/etc that predominantly uses both sides can be called both sided, while one predominantly using one side can be called one sided.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Most other mods don’t have a special name and generally have descriptive names, except for the Brocka (“Brochka” or “Brokka”, depending on where you live) mod. This mod was named after former player Peter Brocka (who is now the mayor of Trnava, Slovakia). A Brocka mod is the removal of material from the inner ankle area, which helps to create a flatter inside surface.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Brand new bags tend to have stiff material that needs to be broken-in. A not broken-in bag will often be rolly and harder to control, while a well broken-in bag will be more floppy or stally.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A trick that’s done on both sides within a single string is called BSOS, which stands for “both sides one string” and is sometimes jokingly written as “bee sauce.” BSOR is sometimes used in place of BSOS, meaning “both sides one run.”
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Contact Types The simplest type of contact is a kick. Toe kicks done with an ankle hinge are sometimes called toe flicks, and knee kicks are sometimes called knee bumps.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Other Contacts Other less common types of contacts are: rolls, where the bag literally rolls from one position to another; pincher, where the bag is grabbed with the back of the knee; burger or stallburger where the bag is trapped between two surfaces (usually with the bag in a Toe Stall and using the other shoe on top); transfer, where the bag is moved from one contact to another; wrap where an Inside Stall is moved into a Clipper (or vice versa); walkover where the stalling foot is placed on the ground and the other steps over it; and rake where the bag is grabbed from one position and dragged to another, most commonly from Xbd to Toe. The bag can also be caught in or set from a rooted position, meaning that the foot the bag is on is on the ground.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Non-Freestyle Footbag Disciplines/Games Hacky sack or circle kicking is the most well-known game of footbag that is about keeping the bag off the ground for as long as possible, usually with a group of people.

passback_explanation: A clean trick or trick component is one performed well and without flaws.

passback_explanation: LIMP tricks are the collection of basic toe tricks: Legover, Illusion, Mirage, and Pickup. CLIMP is an extension of LIMP which includes Clipper. An Everything drill is a drill which uses a single uptime set + each LIMP trick both sides. Ex: Pixie Everything = a drill of Pixie Legover, Pixie ss Legover, Pixie Illusion, Pixie ss Illusion, Pixie Mirage, Pixie ss Mirage, Pixie Pickup, and Pixie ss Pickup, each on both sides. An Everything drill has no set order, and just requires every unique trick be done once.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A combo is any number of tricks linked in a group, usually with some sort of theme or aspect that joins them together. A string could technically be described as a combo, but combos are usually shorter and more purposefully thought-out than strings. A link is specifically a two-trick combo.

passback_explanation: A Clipper is an Inside Stall in a crossbody (xbd) position, meaning that one leg is positioned behind the other. In order to get the flattest possible surface for an Inside Stall or Clipper, you have to bend or crank your ankle in order to flex the inside surface upwards.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Crispy + [base trick] means to take part of the downtime phase of the base trick and “re-time” it into the uptime. For example, a DLO is a downtime Miraging Legover. A “crispy DLO” takes the mirage and moves it from downtime to uptime (making a Blurry Legover), which results in a trick that uses the same motions, but different timing. Crispy is not commonly used, and is (IMO) sometimes longer or more confusing than using the technical name.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A Clipper is an Inside Stall in a crossbody (xbd) position, meaning that one leg is positioned behind the other. In order to get the flattest possible surface for an Inside Stall or Clipper, you have to bend or crank your ankle in order to flex the inside surface upwards.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A well-executed duck is one performed close to the neck, and a duck becomes thinner the farther from the neck it gets. A duck done with just the very top of the head is said to be crowny.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A clean dex is one performed within the dex window, which is the phantom space in the air that the leg draws around the bag. A deep dex is one performed high in the window, further away from the shoe. A thin dex is one performed close to the edge of the window, closer to the shoe, and a very thin dex performed with just a bit of the shoe is sometimes called shoey.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: The most common contact type seen in freestyle is a delay or stall, where the bag is briefly stopped on a surface before being set for the next trick.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A drill is a combo that is done using one side of the body, which then repeats exactly but with the other side of the body. A drill can be notated in string notation with just the unique half and then “rpt” at the repeat point. Ex: Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage>Legover. To notate more or less contacts of a drill either “[N] contacts of [drill]” or “[drill] x[N]” can be used. Ex: 3 contacts of Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage; Mirage>Legover rpt x3 = 12 total contacts.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A marathon drill is a drill that is repeated for a large number of contacts or until a period of time has passed (ex: 100 contacts of Mirage>ss Mirage rpt; 60 seconds of Mirage>ss Mirage rpt). When marathon drilling, drops do not interrupt the contact count (ex: drop on contact 24, next attempt is contact 25). Marathon drilling is often used to train consistency of specific tricks and general endurance.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Sides A component can be further specified with a side to describe exactly which half of the body should perform it. Sides are described relatively to the previous component, and it’s rarely important whether it’s “left” or “right” side, but rather same-side (ss)/near or opposite (op)/far. When thinking about the body as two vertical halves, ss connects two components performed on the same vertical half, while op connects two components performed on one vertical half and then the other.

passback_explanation: Equipment Bags Footbags, or bags for short, are the primary piece of equipment used to play freestyle footbag. Within footbag culture, you’ll mostly hear people refer to them as footbags or bags, but you’ll sometimes hear them referred to as hacks or even hacky sacks (somewhat ironically).

passback_explanation: A pulled or slurry set is one where the bag is dragged through an uptime dex or a spin before the intended component actually happens (ex: a Stepping set where the leg passes over the bag while the bag remains on the set foot). Pulled spins are also sometimes referred to as froggy.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Dex Styles & Fullness Dexes can be done in two movement styles: hippy, where the dex motion comes from the hip and the thigh circles the bag, or leggy, where the dex motion comes from the knee and the calf circles the bag. Every dex can also be described in terms of “fullness”: A full dex is one that circles that bag entirely (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the left). A half dex only circles the bag partially (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the right).

passback_explanation: Ducking: head moves towards the bag, bag falls to the opposite side the head moved Weaving: head moves towards the bag, bag falls to the same side the head moved Diving: head moves over the bag and under it back to the original position, bag falls to the same side as the initial head movement Zulu: head moves over the bag and under it back to the original position, bag falls to the opposite side as the initial head movement Spins Spins are performed by rotating the entire body to various degrees: gyro means to spin 180 degrees, while spinning means to spin 360 degrees. The two terms can be combined, along with double or triple, to create a spin of any half or full degree. Ex: Spinning Gyro = 450 degrees, Gyro Gyro = 180 degrees, then 180 degrees in the other direction.
notes: matches UX1 token: Gyro (rotation modifier; +1)

A half dex only circles the bag partially (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the right).

passback_explanation: Ending a trick or string A string or a trick can be ended in one of a few ways. In footbag, players hit tricks, as opposed to “landing” them. If a player hits a trick and catches the bag, that is a handcatch, and in string notation can be written as [trick]>hand, which usually implies good execution and control. The term seal is also used to imply hitting a trick with enough control to do something after; ex: “Whirl sealed with Mirage.”
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Contact Surfaces The most common contact surfaces are the toe and inside surfaces of the shoe, as well as the less commonly used outside, heel, and sole surfaces. Any non-shoe surface just uses the name for the part of the body, such as knee, calf, or back.

passback_explanation: Events & Competition There are generally two types of footbag events that are hosted: tournaments and jams. Tournaments are focused on competition, with the World Championships (or Worlds) being the biggest tournament of the year. Jams are more casual events, and may still have competition, but are more focused on getting regional players together for a fun time.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: The technical name of a trick is related to its Job’s Notation (technically pronounced “Jobe”, but just as often pronounced “Jahb”), named after Ben Jobe, which is a written form of all of a trick’s components, in which phase they occur, and the sides they are performed on. Ex: Clipper>ss In Dex>>op Out Dex>ss Clipper

passback_explanation: Contact Types The simplest type of contact is a kick. Toe kicks done with an ankle hinge are sometimes called toe flicks, and knee kicks are sometimes called knee bumps.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Rippin’ Run, Iron Man, and Last Man Standing are all variations of “who can do the longest string before dropping.” Rippin’ Run (named after “Rippin” Rick Reese) and Iron Man are head-to-head, while Last Man Standing is every player at the same time.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Shoes Though footbag can be played barefoot or with any kind of shoe, there are certain kinds that are most-suited to the sport. Among the many brands and models used over the years, the two that have most become part of the culture are the Adidas Rod Laver and Quantums. For a long time, from the mid-90s to mid-2010s, Lavers were the standard freestyle shoe, but the specific model that was popular is no longer made. Quantums were the first shoes designed specifically for footbag by the community itself which had a run in the 2010s and have recently been redesigned and released.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Dex Styles & Fullness Dexes can be done in two movement styles: hippy, where the dex motion comes from the hip and the thigh circles the bag, or leggy, where the dex motion comes from the knee and the calf circles the bag. Every dex can also be described in terms of “fullness”: A full dex is one that circles that bag entirely (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the left). A half dex only circles the bag partially (leg starts on the left, circles the bag, and ends on the right).
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: LIMP tricks are the collection of basic toe tricks: Legover, Illusion, Mirage, and Pickup. CLIMP is an extension of LIMP which includes Clipper. An Everything drill is a drill which uses a single uptime set + each LIMP trick both sides. Ex: Pixie Everything = a drill of Pixie Legover, Pixie ss Legover, Pixie Illusion, Pixie ss Illusion, Pixie Mirage, Pixie ss Mirage, Pixie Pickup, and Pixie ss Pickup, each on both sides. An Everything drill has no set order, and just requires every unique trick be done once.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: The typical way that the sport is played is with strings or runs, which are continuous flows of tricks performed in an unplanned “stream of consciousness” style. Tricks performed one after another are said to be linked together. String length is counted in “contacts,” which in this context is used generically to encompass tricks, stalls, kicks, etc. Ex: a 12-contact string.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: To get more energy and rhythm into stalls and sets, the magic hop is used, where the support leg does a small hop just before and just after the bag is stalled on the set foot. The magic hop helps to use less energy and smoothen stalls and sets. It doesn’t seem to be universally agreed upon if the magic hop refers to the hop during “catch” or “set”, or rather a combination of the two.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A marathon drill is a drill that is repeated for a large number of contacts or until a period of time has passed (ex: 100 contacts of Mirage>ss Mirage rpt; 60 seconds of Mirage>ss Mirage rpt). When marathon drilling, drops do not interrupt the contact count (ex: drop on contact 24, next attempt is contact 25). Marathon drilling is often used to train consistency of specific tricks and general endurance.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Doing a trick, or being able to do a trick in the middle of a string is called midstring, which is typically much harder than doing the trick in isolation.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Mirage Race is a joke event where players race towards a finish line but can only move forward by doing the trick Mirage.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A mirror is a link where the two tricks are mirror images of each other when comparing them in forward and reverse motion. For example, Smear>Atomic Legover is a mirror link because if the combo were reversed after the second trick, it would play back as Smear>Atomic Legover (like a palindrome).
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Shoe mods        2
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A muted component is one where an active leg is held in the air for the entirety of a component without putting it on the ground. Like symposium and symple, it’s mostly used for dexes, but can be applied to other components as well. Some combinations of components more naturally lead to something muted (like Clipper>Swirl), while for others doing something muted is a variation (like Pixie ss Mirage).
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Sides A component can be further specified with a side to describe exactly which half of the body should perform it. Sides are described relatively to the previous component, and it’s rarely important whether it’s “left” or “right” side, but rather same-side (ss)/near or opposite (op)/far. When thinking about the body as two vertical halves, ss connects two components performed on the same vertical half, while op connects two components performed on one vertical half and then the other.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Footbag net is a sport played over a net with similar rules to volleyball.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Technical names can get quite long, so many common tricks have nicknames, usually created by the inventor of the trick. For example, “Stepping op Butterfly” has the nickname of “Ripwalk.” Not every trick has a nickname, but even if they do, it’s always valid to use the technical name to describe it.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Within a string or combo, the tricks can be classified as either unique or non-unique, signifying if they’ve been done before on that specific side of the body. For example, Mirage>Legover>Mirage>ss Legover has three unique contacts: two for the Mirages on each side, and one for the Legover on one side. Even though the second Legover is a ss variant, it’s not counted as unique from the previous one because it is fundamentally the same trick.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Both sides or bs refers to doing a trick using both sides of the body. A combo/string/player/etc that predominantly uses both sides can be called both sided, while one predominantly using one side can be called one sided.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Ducking: head moves towards the bag, bag falls to the opposite side the head moved Weaving: head moves towards the bag, bag falls to the same side the head moved Diving: head moves over the bag and under it back to the original position, bag falls to the same side as the initial head movement Zulu: head moves over the bag and under it back to the original position, bag falls to the opposite side as the initial head movement Spins Spins are performed by rotating the entire body to various degrees: gyro means to spin 180 degrees, while spinning means to spin 360 degrees. The two terms can be combined, along with double or triple, to create a spin of any half or full degree. Ex: Spinning Gyro = 450 degrees, Gyro Gyro = 180 degrees, then 180 degrees in the other direction.
notes: matches UX1 token: Opposite side
matches UX1 token: Out (outward arc)

passback_explanation: Contact Surfaces The most common contact surfaces are the toe and inside surfaces of the shoe, as well as the less commonly used outside, heel, and sole surfaces. Any non-shoe surface just uses the name for the part of the body, such as knee, calf, or back.
notes: matches UX1 token: Outside (delay surface)

passback_explanation: A bailed trick is one that was attempted but modified on the fly to something easier to avoid dropping (ex: was going for a Spinning Whirl but bailed to Spinning Clipper because the set was too low). A footed trick is one that was almost hit, but the bag rolled off the stalling foot at the end. A dropped trick could also be over-dexed if the leg dexes too far which causes it to miss the bag entirely.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Bags are constructed with different numbers of panels and these numbers are used to refer to the style of bag, most commonly 32s and 14s, but can be any number. Bags can be filled with various kinds of fill, such as plastic or sand, but most often metal, which gives them enough weight to properly control and feel.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Paradox        5
notes: matches UX1 token: Paradox (body modifier; +1)

passback_explanation: Ducks Ducks are performed by dipping the head in a particular direction and allowing the bag to pass around the neck. Ducking, diving, weaving, and zulu are all variations of the general ducking component that differ in the direction the head moves and where the bag goes after the duck:
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Other Contacts Other less common types of contacts are: rolls, where the bag literally rolls from one position to another; pincher, where the bag is grabbed with the back of the knee; burger or stallburger where the bag is trapped between two surfaces (usually with the bag in a Toe Stall and using the other shoe on top); transfer, where the bag is moved from one contact to another; wrap where an Inside Stall is moved into a Clipper (or vice versa); walkover where the stalling foot is placed on the ground and the other steps over it; and rake where the bag is grabbed from one position and dragged to another, most commonly from Xbd to Toe. The bag can also be caught in or set from a rooted position, meaning that the foot the bag is on is on the ground.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Circles The most common way to play freestyle footbag with others is in a circle: players form a literal circle, usually with 3-5 people, and take individual turns with the bag to do tricks. Play progresses in order, usually clockwise, and when a player’s turn is over they pass the bag to the next player for their turn. After a player’s turn, they will often get props (like a high five) from the others when they do something cool.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Most other mods don’t have a special name and generally have descriptive names, except for the Brocka (“Brochka” or “Brokka”, depending on where you live) mod. This mod was named after former player Peter Brocka (who is now the mayor of Trnava, Slovakia). A Brocka mod is the removal of material from the inner ankle area, which helps to create a flatter inside surface.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A pulled or slurry set is one where the bag is dragged through an uptime dex or a spin before the intended component actually happens (ex: a Stepping set where the leg passes over the bag while the bag remains on the set foot). Pulled spins are also sometimes referred to as froggy.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Within the Fearless and Beastly tiers is the subcategory of “PDX Whirl-Free” (PWF), which means to do those combos without any “Pdx Whirl” component. Pdx Whirl is the simplest 4-ADD component, which makes PWF Fearless/Beastly much more challenging.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Pyramid is a combo-building game: one player does one trick, then the next player has a few attempts (usually 3) to repeat that trick, and add one more trick of their choosing to create the start of a combo. The next player repeats the 2-trick combo and adds one more trick, and so on with each player adding one more trick to create a longer and longer combo. If a player fails to complete the combo + 1 trick, they are eliminated. Play continues until one player remains.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

Among the many brands and models used over the years, the two that have most become part of the culture are the Adidas Rod Laver and Quantums. For a long time, from the mid-90s to mid-2010s, Lavers were the standard freestyle shoe, but the specific model that was popular is no longer made. Quantums were the first shoes designed specifically for footbag by the community itself which had a run in the 2010s and have recently been redesigned and released.

The bag can also be caught in or set from a rooted position, meaning that the foot the bag is on is on the ground.

passback_explanation: A drill is a combo that is done using one side of the body, which then repeats exactly but with the other side of the body. A drill can be notated in string notation with just the unique half and then “rpt” at the repeat point. Ex: Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage>Legover. To notate more or less contacts of a drill either “[N] contacts of [drill]” or “[drill] x[N]” can be used. Ex: 3 contacts of Mirage>Legover rpt = Mirage>Legover>Mirage; Mirage>Legover rpt x3 = 12 total contacts.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Request is an elimination-style event where organizers prepare ahead of time a list of links which players must then complete with no prior knowledge or practice.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A rewind combo is similar to a drill, but at the halfway point, the combo order reverses instead of taking the same order. For example, Legover>ss Mirage>ss Mirage>Legover.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: If the player makes a mistake and the bag lands on the ground, that is a drop or a dropped trick. Any combination of tricks performed without dropping can be said to be dropless; this term is usually applied in a competitive context (ex: a “dropless routine”) to imply a high level of control.

passback_explanation: A trick that ends on a toe stall that is caught with a very arced or circular motion instead of a straight up and down motion is referred to as scooped or scoopy.

passback_explanation: Ending a trick or string A string or a trick can be ended in one of a few ways. In footbag, players hit tricks, as opposed to “landing” them. If a player hits a trick and catches the bag, that is a handcatch, and in string notation can be written as [trick]>hand, which usually implies good execution and control. The term seal is also used to imply hitting a trick with enough control to do something after; ex: “Whirl sealed with Mirage.”
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Within a circle, If a player wants to give the previous one another turn for any reason they can give them a passback. A player might also self-serve to themselves which, depending on who you ask, could be seen as disrespectful to the other players. Another “YMMV” disrespectful move is to toe scrape or toe up the bag by sliding it onto your foot using the sole of the other, which could cause damage to it on rough ground.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A clean dex is one performed within the dex window, which is the phantom space in the air that the leg draws around the bag. A deep dex is one performed high in the window, further away from the shoe. A thin dex is one performed close to the edge of the window, closer to the shoe, and a very thin dex performed with just a bit of the shoe is sometimes called shoey.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Tricks Footbag tricks are performed using a footbag and various parts of the body, most prominently the legs. To practice a trick without the bag, to get a feel for the motions, is to air shred it.

passback_explanation: Shred 30 is a formula-based event where players have 30 seconds to do a combo and are scored on the ADD count and uniqueness of the combo.

passback_explanation: Circle and Battle are variants of a straight-ahead technical showdown. Circle mimics the style of a casual circle, while Battle is 1-on-1. Shred-Off is an online-only async variant of Battle.

passback_explanation: A symple component is one that starts symposium but midway through, the non-symple foot returns to the ground. Like symposium, symple is normally applied to dexes, but can be used for any component that fits the description. A trick that is more naturally done symple can technically be done symposium, but the bag would have to be caught in a rooted position (ex: Legover, Swirl).

passback_explanation: The technical name of a trick is one that uses the simplest named version of every component and relative side to connect them as necessary. Oftentimes, the side is implied by the previous component and is not needed. For example, the technical name of the trick “Phoenix” is Pixie Ducking Butterfly, with “Ducking op Butterfly” being implied.

The first use of a term is bolded, while further uses of it are in normal text. Alphabetical index of all terms can be found at the end of the document.

passback_explanation: Equipment Bags Footbags, or bags for short, are the primary piece of equipment used to play freestyle footbag. Within footbag culture, you’ll mostly hear people refer to them as footbags or bags, but you’ll sometimes hear them referred to as hacks or even hacky sacks (somewhat ironically).
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: ADD Groupings Based on how many ADDs a trick has, it can be grouped and sorted into various tiers: 2-ADD and up tricks are referred to as Tiltless tricks. 3-ADD and up as Guiltless, 4 and up as Tripless, 5 and up as Fearless, 6 and up as Beastly, and 7 and up as Godly. For ADD tiers 2-5, the names can drop the “-less” suffix to describe the tier below them, for example: “Tilts” refers to 1-ADD tricks, “Guilts” 2-ADD, and so on. There are also special names for 3-trick Beastly and Godly combos: a 6>6>6 is called a “Beast”, and a 7>7>7 is called a “Jackpot”.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer
no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Most shoes that are used for footbag need some modifications or mods in order to reach their full potential. The most common one is a toe wall mod, which pulls the toe walls away from the toe surface and creates a toe box that is large and sturdy enough to hold a bag.

passback_explanation: Events & Competition There are generally two types of footbag events that are hosted: tournaments and jams. Tournaments are focused on competition, with the World Championships (or Worlds) being the biggest tournament of the year. Jams are more casual events, and may still have competition, but are more focused on getting regional players together for a fun time.

passback_explanation: Trick phases A single trick can be broken down into three phases depending on which direction the bag is moving at a given time. The uptime of a trick is in the time when the bag is moving up, the downtime is when the bag is moving down, and the midtime is the brief moment in between uptime and midtime when the bag is peaking.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Other Contacts Other less common types of contacts are: rolls, where the bag literally rolls from one position to another; pincher, where the bag is grabbed with the back of the knee; burger or stallburger where the bag is trapped between two surfaces (usually with the bag in a Toe Stall and using the other shoe on top); transfer, where the bag is moved from one contact to another; wrap where an Inside Stall is moved into a Clipper (or vice versa); walkover where the stalling foot is placed on the ground and the other steps over it; and rake where the bag is grabbed from one position and dragged to another, most commonly from Xbd to Toe. The bag can also be caught in or set from a rooted position, meaning that the foot the bag is on is on the ground.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer
matches UX1 token: Weaving (body modifier; +1)

passback_explanation: A clean dex is one performed within the dex window, which is the phantom space in the air that the leg draws around the bag. A deep dex is one performed high in the window, further away from the shoe. A thin dex is one performed close to the edge of the window, closer to the shoe, and a very thin dex performed with just a bit of the shoe is sometimes called shoey.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: Events & Competition There are generally two types of footbag events that are hosted: tournaments and jams. Tournaments are focused on competition, with the World Championships (or Worlds) being the biggest tournament of the year. Jams are more casual events, and may still have competition, but are more focused on getting regional players together for a fun time.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: A Clipper is an Inside Stall in a crossbody (xbd) position, meaning that one leg is positioned behind the other. In order to get the flattest possible surface for an Inside Stall or Clipper, you have to bend or crank your ankle in order to flex the inside surface upwards.
notes: no UX1 token match; candidate for educational glossary layer

passback_explanation: ADD Categories ADDs are counted in whole numbers and are awarded for various components that are each worth 1 ADD: DEL, DEX, XBD (with the same meanings as previously defined), UNS for any contact surfaces apart from Toe, Inside, Outside, and Knee), and BOD for any Spinning, Ducking, Paradox, Symp, Flying, or Xdex component. Xdex is similar to Paradox in that it involves hip pivoting and positioning the body between dexes, but for Toe tricks instead of Xbd ones (ex: the movement in an Atomsmasher after the set to bring the Mirage leg under the bag).
notes: matches UX1 token: X-Dex (component flag; +1)

passback_explanation: Ducks Ducks are performed by dipping the head in a particular direction and allowing the bag to pass around the neck. Ducking, diving, weaving, and zulu are all variations of the general ducking component that differ in the direction the head moves and where the bag goes after the duck:
